g.data-narration: Different scheduling algorithms have different properties. We compare them using five key criteria to determine which is best for a particular situation.
g.text: Five key metrics for evaluating scheduling algorithms

g.data-narration: CPU utilization: keep the CPU as busy as possible. In real systems, ranges from 40 percent for lightly loaded to 90 percent for heavily loaded. Goal is to maximize.
Throughput: number of processes completed per unit of time. For long processes may be one per hour. For short transactions, could be ten per second. Maximize this.
Turnaround time: total time from process submission to completion. Includes waiting in ready queue, executing on CPU, and doing IO. Sum of all intervals. Minimize this.
Waiting time: total time spent waiting in the ready queue. Scheduling only affects waiting time, not execution or IO time. Key metric for algorithm comparison.
g.text: Time spent in ready queue only

g.data-narration: Response time: time from request submission to first response produced. Important for interactive systems. Not total output time, just time to start responding.
Optimization goals: maximize CPU utilization and throughput. Minimize turnaround, waiting, and response time. Usually optimize the average, sometimes minimize variance.
No single algorithm optimizes all criteria. The choice depends on the system type: batch systems favor throughput, interactive systems favor response time.
g.text: Maximize: CPU util + throughput | Minimize: turnaround + waiting + response
